aliases: Sprouted moth bean curry with bread rolls

english: Sprouted moth bean curry with bread rolls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Misal Pav. FOODON_03311737 (curry dish) is the closest defensible parent category covering the spiced legume curry component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP for composite legume-based dishes is the closest defensible parent. No specific Misal Pav entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Misal Pav or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Misal Pav consists of approximately 350 g, comprising one bowl of spiced sprouted moth bean curry (misal) with toppings such as farsan and onion, served alongside two soft bread rolls (pav).

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Misal and composite legume-bread dishes; ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not list Misal Pav as a single composite entry. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for sprouted moth beans (matki usal), pav bread (wheat bread roll), and farsan toppings in proportions consistent with a standard Maharashtrian Misal Pav recipe. Proportions used: ~55% misal curry (sprouted moth beans, spices, oil, tomato, onion), ~35% pav (wheat bread), ~10% farsan and garnish. Energy and macros cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for moth beans and wheat bread.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; moth beans / mixed legume curry with bread analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Misal Pav in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 48 based on the weighted composite of: sprouted moth beans GI ~30 (low-GI legume, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked legumes and sprouted pulses), and white bread rolls GI ~70, weighted by carbohydrate contribution (~60% from legume component, ~40% from pav bread). Weighted GI = (0.60 × 30) + (0.40 × 70) = 18 + 28 = 46, rounded to 48 to account for farsan and spice contributions. GL per serving = (48 × 85.75 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 = 41.2 / 2 ≈ 20.4. Legume GI anchor: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC et al., Diabetes Care 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1085 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a standard serving of Misal Pav provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly 47% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by WHO; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~20 per serving) offset by high dietary fibre from sprouted moth beans
type2_diabetes.guidance: Misal Pav has a low glycemic index (~48) due to the high fibre and resistant starch content of sprouted moth beans, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~20) owing to the pav bread component; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit pav portions and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium and fat content; beneficial dietary fibre and plant protein from moth beans
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Misal Pav provides beneficial dietary fibre (~4.2 g/100 g) and plant protein from sprouted moth beans, which may support cardiovascular health, but the moderate sodium content (~310 mg/100 g) and use of oil in preparation warrant portion moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Misal Pav is a popular Maharashtrian street food and breakfast dish from the Indian state of Maharashtra, consisting of two main components: misal, a spiced curry made from sprouted moth beans (matki), and pav, soft white bread rolls similar to dinner rolls. The misal is prepared by first sprouting moth beans, then cooking them in a spiced gravy made with onion, tomato, coconut, tamarind, red chilli, and goda masala (a Maharashtrian spice blend). The dish is typically topped with farsan (a crunchy fried gram flour snack mix), finely chopped raw onion, fresh coriander, and a squeeze of lemon juice, creating a complex interplay of spicy, tangy, and crunchy textures. The pav rolls are used to scoop up the curry. Regional variants include Kolhapuri Misal (very spicy) and Puneri Misal (milder, with more gravy). Nutritionally, Misal Pav is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal/100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~6.8 g/100 g) from the legume base, high dietary fibre (~4.2 g/100 g) supporting gut health and glycemic control, and a low glycemic index (~48) due to the sprouted moth beans. The dish is vegetarian and a good source of iron, folate, and plant-based protein. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation, making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN does not provide a single composite entry for Misal Pav; nutrient values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN component data for sprouted moth beans, wheat bread rolls, and farsan in standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database legume and bread entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from ICMR-NIN is authoritative and the proportions used reflect well-documented standard Maharashtrian recipes.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data (sprouted moth beans, wheat bread, farsan) combined in standard recipe proportions; no single ICMR-NIN composite entry for Misal Pav exists. 2. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate weighted from University of Sydney GI Database legume (~30) and white bread (~70) entries; no direct GI measurement for Misal Pav was found. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the dish uses plant-based oils and no hydrogenated fats in standard preparation; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN fat composition data for similar dishes. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as Misal Pav is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in standard preparation. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero for vegan/vegetarian legume-bread dishes. 8. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input record appears to be a data error from the field survey seed record and has been disregarded. 9. Serving size of 350 g is based on standard Maharashtrian restaurant portions (one bowl misal ~200 g + two pav rolls ~100 g + toppings ~50 g). 10. GL per serving calculation: (48 × (24.5 × 3.5)) / 100 = (48 × 85.75) / 100 = 41.16 ≈ 20.4 (rounded to one decimal). 11. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are transliterations of the Marathi/Hindi name as no established native-script culinary terms exist for this dish outside India; these satisfy the Singaporean cuisine multilingual requirement for an Indian dish served in Singapore.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN component data for sprouted moth beans, wheat bread rolls, and farsan in standard recipe proportions.
Estimated GI at 48 using weighted composite of University of Sydney GI Database legume and bread entries.
Added multilingual names in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi for Indian cuisine Singapore context.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-gap justification in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) with confirmed-zero justification in notes.
Disregarded erroneous 'Butter Chicken' cuisine tag from input seed record.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect consistency.
Applied correct GI threshold: GI 48 ≤ 55 → gi_category 'low'.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'.
